name: Prashna Upanishad
summary: Six students approach the sage Pippalada with six profound questions (prashnas) about the origin of creation, prana, sleep and waking, the syllable Om, the five pranas, and the sixteen-part Purusha. Pippalada answers each in turn.

verses.sanskrit: स होवाच — प्राणश्च रयिश्च प्रजापतिः सृष्टौ — तयोरन्यतरः प्राणः — आदित्यः ||१.४||
verses.transliteration: sa hovāca — prāṇaś ca rayiś ca prajāpatiḥ sṛṣṭau — tayor anyataraḥ prāṇaḥ — ādityaḥ ||1.4||
verses.english_translation: He said: Prajapati created prana and rayi (matter). Of these two, prana is the Sun.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: प्राणः — चेतनः सूर्यः। रयिः — अचेतना चन्द्रमाः। एतौ द्वौ सर्वस्य जगतः कारणम्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Prana is the conscious solar principle; rayi is the inert lunar principle. These two are the cause of the entire world — the active and passive aspects of creation.

verses.sanskrit: ओमित्येतदक्षरमभिध्यायीत — एष ह वाव साधु कर्म करोति यः एनमभिध्यायीत ||५.२||
verses.transliteration: om ity etad akṣaram abhidhyāyīta — eṣa ha vāva sādhu karma karoti yaḥ enam abhidhyāyīta ||5.2||
verses.english_translation: One should meditate on this syllable Om. Whoever meditates on it does good action.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: ओम् इति — प्रणवः परब्रह्मणः वाचकः। तम् अभिध्यायन् — उपासन् — साधु कर्म करोति — श्रेयः प्राप्नोति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Om is the indicator of the supreme Brahman. One who meditates on Om attains the good — both the lower good of higher worlds and the highest good of liberation, depending on the depth of meditation.

verses.sanskrit: कस्मिन्नु भगवो विज्ञाते सर्वमिदं विज्ञातं भवतीति ||६.१||
verses.transliteration: kasmin nu bhagavo vijñāte sarvam idaṃ vijñātaṃ bhavatīti ||6.1||
verses.english_translation: By knowing what, O Blessed One, does all this become known?
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: कस्मिन् विज्ञाते — किं एकं ज्ञात्वा सर्वं ज्ञातं भवति — इति षष्ठः प्रश्नः।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: This is the sixth and final question: by knowing what one thing does everything become known? The answer points to the Self (Atman) as the ground of all knowledge.

verses.sanskrit: आदित्यो ह वै प्राणः | रयिरेव चन्द्रमाः | यद्धैतत्सर्वं मूर्तं चामूर्तं च रयिरेव तत् ||१.५||
verses.transliteration: ādityo ha vai prāṇaḥ | rayir eva candramāḥ | yad dhaitad sarvaṃ mūrtaṃ cāmūrtaṃ ca rayir eva tat ||1.5||
verses.english_translation: The Sun is indeed prana; the Moon is indeed rayi. Whatever is formed and formless — all that is rayi alone.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: आदित्यः — सूर्यः — प्राणः — चेतनः। चन्द्रमाः — रयिः — अचेतनः। मूर्तम् अमूर्तम् — स्थूलसूक्ष्मम् — सर्वम् रयिः।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The Sun represents the conscious, active principle (prana); the Moon represents the material, passive principle (rayi). All formed and formless matter is rayi. This cosmic duality underlies all creation.

verses.sanskrit: कति ह वा अस्मिन् पुरुषे प्राणाः | कतर एते पुरुषं विधारयन्ति | कश्चैतेषां वरिष्ठ इति ||२.१||
verses.transliteration: kati ha vā asmin puruṣe prāṇāḥ | katara ete puruṣaṃ vidhārayanti | kaś caitesāṃ variṣṭha iti ||2.1||
verses.english_translation: How many pranas are there in this person? Which of these support the person? And which among them is the greatest?
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: कति प्राणाः — कियन्तः प्राणाः अस्मिन् शरीरे। कतरे विधारयन्ति — के शरीरं धारयन्ति। कः वरिष्ठः — कः प्रधानः।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The second question asks about the pranas — how many are there, which ones sustain the body, and which is the chief. Pippalada will answer that there are five pranas and that the chief prana (mukhya prana) is supreme.

verses.sanskrit: पञ्चैव प्राणाः | प्राणोऽपानो व्यान उदानः समानश्चेति ||२.३||
verses.transliteration: pañcaiva prāṇāḥ | prāṇo'pāno vyāna udānaḥ samānaś ceti ||2.3||
verses.english_translation: There are five pranas: prana, apana, vyana, udana, and samana.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: पञ्च प्राणाः — प्राणः — नासिकाग्रे वर्तते। अपानः — पायौ। व्यानः — सर्वशरीरे। उदानः — कण्ठे। समानः — नाभौ।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The five pranas have different locations and functions. Prana operates at the nostrils (inhalation). Apana operates at the lower body (elimination). Vyana pervades the whole body. Udana operates at the throat (speech, death). Samana operates at the navel (digestion).

verses.sanskrit: एष ह वाव वरिष्ठः यः प्राणः | एष एव सर्वाणि भूतानि धारयति ||२.१३||
verses.transliteration: eṣa ha vāva variṣṭhaḥ yaḥ prāṇaḥ | eṣa eva sarvāṇi bhūtāni dhārayati ||2.13||
verses.english_translation: This one is indeed the greatest — the chief prana. This one alone supports all beings.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: एषः प्राणः — मुख्यप्राणः — वरिष्ठः — सर्वेषाम् प्रधानः। एषः सर्वाणि भूतानि धारयति — सर्वेषाम् जीवनाधारः।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The chief prana (mukhya prana) is the greatest — it is the life-force that sustains all beings. All the other pranas are its manifestations. Without the chief prana, no being can live.

verses.sanskrit: अकार उ वै सर्वा वाक् | सैषा स्पर्शोष्मभिर्व्यज्यमाना बहुधा भवति ||५.५||
verses.transliteration: akāra u vai sarvā vāk | saiṣā sparśoṣmabhir vyajyamānā bahudhā bhavati ||5.5||

word_meanings.meaning: the letter A is all speech

verses.english_translation: The letter A is indeed all speech. Being manifested by consonants and sibilants, it becomes manifold.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: अकारः — सर्वासाम् वर्णानाम् आधारः। सर्वा वाक् — सर्वे शब्दाः — अकारात् एव।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The letter A is the basis of all speech — all sounds are modifications of A. Similarly, Om (which begins with A) is the basis of all Vedic knowledge. This explains why Om is the most fundamental mantra.

verses.sanskrit: यथा नद्यः स्यन्दमानाः समुद्रेऽस्तं गच्छन्ति | नामरूपे विहाय | तथा विद्वान् नामरूपाद्विमुक्तः परात्परं पुरुषमुपैति दिव्यम् ||६.५||
verses.transliteration: yathā nadyaḥ syandamānāḥ samudre'staṃ gacchanti | nāmarūpe vihāya | tathā vidvān nāmarūpād vimuktaḥ parātparaṃ puruṣam upaiti divyam ||6.5||
verses.english_translation: As rivers flowing merge into the ocean, leaving behind name and form — so the wise one, freed from name and form, attains the divine Person who is beyond the beyond.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: नद्यः — समुद्रे लीयन्ते — नामरूपे विहाय। तथा विद्वान् — ब्रह्मज्ञः — नामरूपात् विमुक्तः — परात्परम् पुरुषम् उपैति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Just as rivers lose their individual names and forms when they merge into the ocean, the wise one loses individual identity when merging into Brahman. This is liberation — the dissolution of the separate self into the infinite.

verses.sanskrit: एष ह वाव वरिष्ठः यः प्राणः | एष एव सर्वाणि भूतानि धारयति ||२.१३||
verses.transliteration: eṣa ha vāva variṣṭhaḥ yaḥ prāṇaḥ | eṣa eva sarvāṇi bhūtāni dhārayati ||2.13||
verses.english_translation: This one is indeed the greatest — the chief prana. This one alone supports all beings.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: एषः प्राणः — मुख्यप्राणः — वरिष्ठः। एषः सर्वाणि भूतानि धारयति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The chief prana is the greatest — it is the life-force that sustains all beings. All the other pranas are its manifestations.

verses.sanskrit: यदा सुप्तः स्वप्नं न कञ्चन पश्यति | अथास्मिन् प्राण एवैकधा भवति ||४.२||
verses.transliteration: yadā suptas svapnaṃ na kañcana paśyati | athāsmin prāṇa evaikadha bhavati ||4.2||
verses.english_translation: When the sleeper sees no dream at all, then he becomes unified in prana alone.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: स्वप्नम् न पश्यति — सुषुप्तौ — प्राणे एकधा भवति — सर्वे प्राणाः मुख्यप्राणे लीयन्ते।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: In deep dreamless sleep, all the individual pranas merge into the chief prana. The senses withdraw, the mind rests, and consciousness becomes unified. This is the daily experience of the deep sleep state.

verses.sanskrit: यथाग्नेः क्षुद्रा विस्फुलिङ्गा व्युच्चरन्त्येवमेवास्मादात्मनः सर्वे प्राणाः | सर्वे लोकाः सर्वे देवाः सर्वाणि भूतानि ||४.८||
verses.transliteration: yathāgneḥ kṣudrā visphuliṅgā vyuccaranty evam evāsmād ātmanaḥ sarve prāṇāḥ | sarve lokāḥ sarve devāḥ sarvāṇi bhūtāni ||4.8||
verses.english_translation: As sparks fly out from fire, so from this Self all pranas, all worlds, all gods, and all beings fly out.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: अग्नेः विस्फुलिङ्गाः — अग्निसरूपाः — व्युच्चरन्ति। तथा आत्मनः — सर्वे प्राणाः — सर्वे लोकाः — सर्वाणि भूतानि — उत्पद्यन्ते।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Just as sparks of the same nature as fire fly out from fire, so from the Self all pranas, worlds, gods, and beings arise. This is the same teaching as the Mundaka — the universe arises from Brahman like sparks from fire.

verses.sanskrit: स यदि विद्वान् साधुकर्मा भवति | तेजसैव लोकानुत्क्रामति ||५.७||
verses.transliteration: sa yadi vidvān sādhukarmā bhavati | tejasaiva lokān utkrāmati ||5.7||

word_meanings.meaning: one who is learned and does good deeds

verses.english_translation: If one is learned and does good deeds, by light alone one rises above the worlds.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: विद्वान् — ओंकारम् जानाति — साधुकर्मा — शुभकर्मकारी — तेजसा — ओंकारतेजसा — लोकान् उत्क्रामति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: One who meditates on Om and performs good deeds rises above all worlds by the light of Om. The meditation on Om combined with right action leads to liberation.

verses.sanskrit: अरा इव रथनाभौ प्राणे सर्वं प्रतिष्ठितम् | ऋचो यजूंषि सामानि यज्ञः क्षत्रं ब्रह्म च ||२.६||
verses.transliteration: arā iva rathanābhau prāṇe sarvaṃ pratiṣṭhitam | ṛco yajūṃṣi sāmāni yajñaḥ kṣatraṃ brahma ca ||2.6||
verses.english_translation: As spokes are established in a wheel hub, so everything is established in prana — the Rig, Yajur, Sama Vedas, sacrifice, power, and Brahman.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: अराः रथनाभौ — नाभौ अराः प्रतिष्ठिताः। तथा प्राणे — सर्वम् — वेदाः यज्ञाः — प्रतिष्ठितम्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Just as all spokes of a wheel are established in the hub, so all the Vedas, sacrifices, and all of existence are established in prana (Brahman). Prana is the hub of the wheel of existence.

verses.transliteration: etāvad evāham etat paraṃ brahma veda | nātaḥ param astīti ||6.7||
verses.english_translation: 'Only this much do I know of this supreme Brahman. There is nothing beyond this.'
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: एतावत् — इयत्परिमाणम् — परम् ब्रह्म वेद — जानामि। नातः परम् — अस्मात् उत्तरम् — नास्ति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Pippalada humbly says 'I know only this much of Brahman — there is nothing beyond this.' This is the concluding statement of the Prashna Upanishad. The teacher acknowledges the limits of verbal teaching — Brahman in its fullness transcends all description.

verses.sanskrit: सुकेशा च भारद्वाजः शैब्यश्च सत्यकामः सौर्यायणी च गार्ग्यः कौसल्यश्चाश्वलायनो भार्गवो वैदर्भिः कबन्धी कात्यायनस्ते हैते ब्रह्मपरा ब्रह्मनिष्ठाः परं ब्रह्मान्वेषमाणा एष ह वै तत्सर्वं वक्ष्यतीति पिप्पलादमुपसन्नाः ||१.१||
verses.transliteration: sukeśā ca bhāradvājaḥ śaibyaś ca satyakāmaḥ sauryāyaṇī ca gārgyaḥ kausalyaś cāśvalāyano bhārgavo vaidarbhiḥ kabandhī kātyāyanas te haite brahmaparā brahmanisṭhāḥ paraṃ brahmānveṣamāṇā eṣa ha vai tat sarvaṃ vakṣyatīti pippalādam upasannāḥ ||1.1||

word_meanings.meaning: devoted to Brahman, established in Brahman

verses.english_translation: Sukesa Bharadvaja, Saibya Satyakama, Sauryayani Gargya, Kausalya Ashvalayana, Bhargava Vaidarbhi, and Kabandhi Katyayana — these, devoted to Brahman and established in Brahman, seeking the supreme Brahman, approached Pippalada.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: षट् शिष्याः — ब्रह्मपराः — परम् ब्रह्म अन्वेषमाणाः — पिप्पलादम् उपसन्नाः।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Six qualified students, all devoted to Brahman and seeking the supreme truth, approached the sage Pippalada. Their qualifications — devotion to Brahman and establishment in Brahman — show they were ready for the highest teaching.

verses.sanskrit: तान् ह स ऋषिरुवाच | भूय एव तपसा ब्रह्मचर्येण श्रद्धया संवत्सरं संवत्स्यथ | यथाकामं प्रश्नान् पृच्छत | यदि विज्ञास्यामः सर्वं ह वो वक्ष्याम इति ||१.२||
verses.transliteration: tān ha sa ṛṣir uvāca | bhūya eva tapasā brahmacaryeṇa śraddhayā saṃvatsaraṃ saṃvatsyatha | yathākāmaṃ praśnān pṛcchata | yadi vijñāsyāmaḥ sarvaṃ ha vo vakṣyāma iti ||1.2||
verses.english_translation: The sage said to them: 'Live here for a year with austerity, celibacy, and faith. Then ask questions as you wish. If I know, I will tell you everything.'
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: तपसा ब्रह्मचर्येण श्रद्धया — एतैः त्रिभिः — संवत्सरम् वसत। ततः प्रश्नान् पृच्छत।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Pippalada first tests the students by asking them to live for a year with austerity, celibacy, and faith. Only after this preparation does he agree to answer their questions. This shows that spiritual knowledge requires preparation — it cannot be given to the unprepared.

verses.sanskrit: अथ कबन्धी कात्यायन उपेत्य पप्रच्छ | भगवन् कुतो ह वा इमाः प्रजाः प्रजायन्त इति ||१.३||
verses.transliteration: atha kabandhī kātyāyana upetya papraccha | bhagavan kuto ha vā imāḥ prajāḥ prajāyanta iti ||1.3||
verses.english_translation: Then Kabandhi Katyayana approached and asked: 'O Blessed One, from where are all these beings born?'
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: कबन्धी — प्रथमः प्रश्नः — कुतः प्रजाः प्रजायन्ते — सृष्टेः कारणम् किम्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The first question is about the origin of creation. Kabandhi asks: from where do all beings arise? This is the fundamental cosmological question that the Upanishad addresses through the teaching of prana and rayi.

verses.sanskrit: स होवाच | प्रजाकामो वाव प्रजापतिस्तपोऽतप्यत | स तपस्तप्त्वा मिथुनमुत्पादयते | रयिं च प्राणं चेत्येतौ मे बहुधा प्रजाः करिष्यत इति ||१.४||
verses.transliteration: sa hovāca | prajākāmo vāva prajāpatis tapo'tapyata | sa tapas taptvā mithunam utpādayate | rayiṃ ca prāṇaṃ cety etau me bahudhā prajāḥ kariṣyata iti ||1.4||
verses.english_translation: He said: 'Prajapati, desiring offspring, performed austerity. Having performed austerity, he produced a pair — rayi and prana — thinking: these two will produce beings for me in many ways.'
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: प्रजापतिः — तपः अतप्यत — मिथुनम् — रयिम् प्राणम् च — उत्पादयत।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Prajapati (the Lord of creation) performed austerity and produced the primordial pair — rayi (matter/moon) and prana (consciousness/sun). All creation arises from the interaction of these two principles.

verses.sanskrit: आदित्यो ह वै प्राणः | रयिरेव चन्द्रमाः | रयिर्वा एतत्सर्वं यन्मूर्तं चामूर्तं च | तस्मान्मूर्तिरेव रयिः ||१.५||
verses.transliteration: ādityo ha vai prāṇaḥ | rayir eva candramāḥ | rayir vā etat sarvaṃ yan mūrtaṃ cāmūrtaṃ ca | tasmān mūrtir eva rayiḥ ||1.5||
verses.english_translation: The Sun is indeed prana; the Moon is indeed rayi. Whatever is formed and formless — all that is rayi. Therefore form itself is rayi.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: आदित्यः — सूर्यः — प्राणः — चेतनः। चन्द्रमाः — रयिः — अचेतनः। मूर्तम् अमूर्तम् — सर्वम् रयिः।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The Sun represents the conscious, active principle (prana); the Moon represents the material, passive principle (rayi). All formed and formless matter is rayi. This cosmic duality underlies all creation.

verses.sanskrit: अथ यः प्राणः स एव संवत्सरः | तस्यायने दक्षिणं चोत्तरं च ||१.९||
verses.transliteration: atha yaḥ prāṇaḥ sa eva saṃvatsaraḥ | tasyāyane dakṣiṇaṃ cottaraṃ ca ||1.9||
verses.english_translation: That which is prana is indeed the year. Its two paths are the southern and the northern.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: प्राणः — संवत्सरः — कालस्वरूपः। दक्षिणायनम् — पितृयानम्। उत्तरायणम् — देवयानम्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Prana manifests as time — the year. The year has two paths: the southern path (pitriyana — path of the ancestors, leading to rebirth) and the northern path (devayana — path of the gods, leading to liberation). This connects cosmic time with the paths after death.

verses.sanskrit: अथ भार्गवो वैदर्भिः पप्रच्छ | भगवन् कति देवाः प्रजां विधारयन्ते | कतर एतत्प्रकाशयन्ते | कश्चैतेषां वरिष्ठ इति ||२.१||
verses.transliteration: atha bhārgavo vaidarbhiḥ papraccha | bhagavan kati devāḥ prajāṃ vidhārayante | katara etat prakāśayante | kaś caitesāṃ variṣṭha iti ||2.1||
verses.english_translation: Then Bhargava Vaidarbhi asked: 'O Blessed One, how many gods support the creatures? Which of these illuminate it? And who among them is the greatest?'
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: द्वितीयः प्रश्नः — कति देवाः — प्रजाम् विधारयन्ते — कः वरिष्ठः।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The second question asks about the cosmic forces that sustain life. Pippalada will answer that the five pranas (along with the senses) support creatures, and the chief prana is the greatest among them.

verses.sanskrit: ते ह देवाः प्राणे विवदमाना ब्रह्म जग्मुः | ते होचुः को नः श्रेष्ठ इति | तान् होवाच यस्मिन् वः प्रयत्युत्क्रान्ते शरीरं पापिष्ठतरमिव दृश्यते स वः श्रेष्ठ इति ||२.२||
verses.transliteration: te ha devāḥ prāṇe vivadamānā brahma jagmuḥ | te hocuḥ ko naḥ śreṣṭha iti | tān hovāca yasmin vaḥ prayaty utkrānte śarīraṃ pāpiṣṭhataram iva dṛśyate sa vaḥ śreṣṭha iti ||2.2||

word_meanings.meaning: who among us is the greatest

verses.english_translation: The gods, disputing about prana, went to Brahman and asked: 'Who among us is the greatest?' He said: 'The one whose departure makes the body appear most wretched — that one is the greatest among you.'
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: देवाः — वाग्आदयः — विवदमानाः — ब्रह्म जग्मुः। ब्रह्म उवाच — यस्मिन् उत्क्रान्ते — शरीरम् पापिष्ठतरम् — स श्रेष्ठः।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The senses disputed about who was greatest. Brahman gave them a test: whichever one, when it departs, makes the body most wretched — that one is the greatest. This leads to the demonstration that the chief prana is supreme.

verses.sanskrit: सा ह वाग्उत्क्रामत् | सा संवत्सरं प्रोष्य आगत्योवाच | कथमशकत मद्विना जीवितुमिति | प्राणा ऊचुः | यथा मूका अप्राणन्तः प्राणेन जीवन्तः ||२.४||
verses.transliteration: sā ha vāg utkrāmat | sā saṃvatsaraṃ proṣya āgatyovāca | katham aśakata mad vinā jīvitum iti | prāṇā ūcuḥ | yathā mūkā aprāṇantaḥ prāṇena jīvantaḥ ||2.4||

word_meanings.meaning: how could you live without me

verses.english_translation: Speech departed. Having stayed away a year, it returned and asked: 'How could you live without me?' The pranas said: 'Like the mute — not speaking, but living by prana.'
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: वाक् उत्क्रामत् — संवत्सरम् — शरीरम् मूकम् अभवत् — किन्तु जीवत् एव। प्राणेन जीवन्तः।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: When speech departed, the body became mute but continued to live. This shows speech is not essential for life. Similarly, when the eye departed, the body became blind but lived. Only when the chief prana departed did the body begin to die — proving prana is the greatest.

verses.sanskrit: अथ कौसल्यश्चाश्वलायनः पप्रच्छ | भगवन् कुत एष प्राणो जायते | कथमायात्यस्मिञ्शरीरे | आत्मानं वा प्रविभज्य कथं प्रतिष्ठते | केनोत्क्रामते | कथं बाह्यमभिधत्ते | कथमध्यात्ममिति ||३.१||
verses.transliteration: atha kausalyaś cāśvalāyanaḥ papraccha | bhagavan kuta eṣa prāṇo jāyate | katham āyāty asmiñ śarīre | ātmānaṃ vā pravibhajya kathaṃ pratiṣṭhate | kenotkrāmate | kathaṃ bāhyam abhidhatte | katham adhyātmam iti ||3.1||
verses.english_translation: Then Kausalya Ashvalayana asked: 'O Blessed One, from where is this prana born? How does it come into this body? How does it establish itself by dividing itself? By what does it depart? How does it relate to the external? How to the internal?'
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: तृतीयः प्रश्नः — प्राणस्य उत्पत्तिः — आगमनम् — विभागः — उत्क्रमणम्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The third question is the most detailed — asking about prana's origin, entry into the body, distribution within it, departure, and its relationship to the external and internal worlds. Pippalada will explain all aspects of prana's functioning.
